meaning: To choose not to participate in something or to leave a group
example: If you don't like the service, you can opt out at any time.
example2: He chose to opt out of the data sharing program.
example3: The email includes a link for users who wish to opt out.
Explanation: The phrase 'opt out' refers to the action of withdrawing from a system or refusing a service that is otherwise provided by default. In IT, it is most commonly seen in the context of 'unsubscribing' from emails or disabling 'tracking cookies'. When a system is set up as 'opt-out', it means the user is automatically included unless they take an action to stop it. Privacy advocates often prefer 'opt-in' systems because 'opt-out' systems rely on users being busy or unaware to keep them enrolled. It is an essential feature for any ethical software service.
usageTip: Use 'opt out' to describe the action of leaving or declining a default setting.
commonMistake: Confusing 'opt out' with 'delete account'; you can opt out of emails while keeping your account.

meaning: To choose not to participate in something or to leave a program
example: The software allows users to opt out of data collection.
example2: If you don't like the new rules, you can opt out of the club.
example3: He chose to opt out of the company's weekend trip.
Explanation: Opting out is the act of removing yourself from a situation or a list. If you are already part of something (often by default), you 'opt out' to stop being part of it. A common example is the 'Unsubscribe' link in an email, which is an 'opt out' mechanism. In many countries, laws require that users have a clear and easy way to opt out of services they no longer want.
commonContext: Email marketing, privacy, and social groups
usageTip: Use 'opt out' when you want to stop receiving something or stop being a member.
commonMistake: Using 'opt out' when you were never 'in' to begin with (use 'decline' instead).
